sections.heading: Overview
sections.content:
Cortex exposes its index through a set of MCP tools. Agents connect via stdio transport and call these tools to build, query, and document a project's code index. All tools take `project_root` as their first argument and return plain text. Errors are returned as `ERROR: {message}` — tools never raise exceptions to the caller.

The tools fall into four groups: **index management**, **querying**, **staleness tracking**, and **documentation**.

sections.heading: Tool Output Standards
sections.content:
All cortex tools follow these output conventions.  When a tool deviates, it is a bug.

**1. Every list operation has a count header**
Any tool that can return more than one result emits a header line before the data:
```
[20 of 87 results — fts ranked]
[5 of 5 nodes — level 1]
[12 of 34 undocumented nodes]
```
This lets an agent see the total without counting lines.

**2. No silent truncation — capped results say so**
If `max_results` cuts the list short, the header says exactly what to do:
```
[20 of 87 results]  (cap=20; pass offset=20 for next page)
```
Tools that return the full set still show the count so the agent knows it is complete.

**3. Every collection-returning tool has `max_results` and `offset`**
`cortex_list`, `cortex_render` (no node_id), and `cortex_list_undocumented` all default to `max_results=60`. Pass a higher value explicitly for full-inventory audits. Use `offset` to page: `offset=60` returns rows 61–120.

**4. Summary-first, detail on request**
Tools with potentially large output default to the smallest useful form. `cortex_check` emits one summary line + problems only by default; pass `problems_only=False` for everything. `cortex_render` at level 1 (ID + summary) is the default orientation form — use level 2 only when you need the full docstring.

**5. Function-level nodes always include location**
Every tool that renders a node list or graph appends `@ path/file.py#L10-L45` for function-level nodes (those with a line range in their location field). This applies to: `cortex_list`, `cortex_render`, `cortex_search`, `cortex_graph` (all nodes in the tree), and `cortex_render_doc` (linked-nodes section).

**6. Filters are substring-based for human-facing params**
Parameters a human or agent will type — `location`, query strings — use substring or FTS matching. Exact match is only required for programmatic IDs like `node_id`. `node_type` accepts friendly aliases `"function"` and `"module"` in addition to the internal names.

sections.heading: Index Management
sections.content:
`cortex_build(project_root)` — Adds newly-discovered nodes/edges to the index. This is **always discovery-only**: existing nodes are never touched, so `DESC_STALE` / `STRUCTURAL_DRIFT` signals are always preserved. Edges are updated in all cases.

A full rebuild (which resets `code_hash` baselines and clears staleness) is intentionally **not available through this tool**. Use the CLI `cortex build --full` for that, after you have resolved and verified all stale nodes.

Returns: `nodes added`, `nodes unchanged`, `edges updated`, `edges unchanged`, and any ontology warnings.

sections.heading: Querying: Render
sections.content:
`cortex_render(project_root, level, node_id=None, max_results=60, offset=0)` — Renders one or all nodes at a chosen detail level.

- Level 0: IDs only — use for a quick inventory
- Level 1: ID + one-line summary + file location for function nodes (`@ path/file.py#L10-L45`) — the default for orientation
- Level 2: full detail including level_2 documentation body and location
- Level 3: step markers only (dFlow-annotated workflows)

Pass a `node_id` to render a single node (no cap).  Omit `node_id` to render all nodes; output starts with `[N of M nodes — level N]` and is capped at `max_results` (default 60). Use `offset` to page.

sections.heading: Querying: List and Graph
sections.content:
`cortex_list(project_root, node_type, tag, parent_id, location, max_results=60, offset=0)` — Lists nodes with optional filters.  Pass `location` (file path substring) to list all nodes in a file in one call — e.g. `location="snakemake_gen.py"`.  Pass `parent_id` to enumerate children via one-hop `composes` edges — works for both module nodes (returns their functions) and doc file nodes (returns their section nodes).  `node_type` accepts `"function"` and `"module"` aliases.  Output: `[N of M results]` header, then one line per node with `@ path#L10-L45` for function nodes.  Use `offset` to page.

`cortex_graph(project_root, node_id, direction, depth)` — Traverses edges from a node. `direction` is `"out"`, `"in"`, or `"both"`. All nodes in the output tree include `@ path#L10-L45` for function-level nodes, so agents can jump straight to `read_file` without a follow-up lookup.

sections.heading: Querying: Search
sections.content:
`cortex_search(project_root, query, level=None, max_results=20, node_type=None)` — Full-text search over node `level_1` and `level_2` fields. Returns one line per match with a `[N of M results — mode]` count header. Each function-node line includes `@ path#L10-L45` so agents can jump straight to `read_file` without a follow-up lookup.

**Query stages** (stops at first stage that returns results):
1. **FTS ranked** — BM25-ranked SQLite FTS5. Single word, quoted phrase (`"scan module"`), prefix (`scan*`), or ALL-words-required multi-word. Most queries stop here.
2. **LIKE-AND fallback** — substring scan; all tokens must appear somewhere in the text. Fires when FTS returns nothing.
3. **LIKE-OR fallback** — substring scan; any token matches. Broad and low-confidence; always capped at 10 regardless of `max_results`. The header says `LIKE-OR fallback (broad, low-confidence)` when this fires — usually means the query is too generic; narrow to a single specific term.

The header labels the mode so agents know whether results are ranked or broad:
```
[20 of 87 results — fts ranked]
[5 of 5 results — LIKE-AND fallback]
[10 of 10 results — LIKE-OR fallback (broad, low-confidence)]
```

**Parameters:**
- `level=1` — search `level_1` (one-line summaries) only; use when you know the symbol name
- `level=2` — search `level_2` (full docstrings) only; use when looking for described behaviour
- `level=None` (default) — search both fields
- `max_results` — cap on returned rows (default 20); LIKE-OR is always capped at 10. Note: `cortex_search` has no `offset` parameter — if you need results beyond the cap, narrow the query or raise `max_results`.
- `node_type` — restrict to `"atomic_process"` (functions) or `"composite_process"` (modules); aliases `"function"` and `"module"` are **not** supported here, use the full type names.

sections.heading: Querying: Source
sections.content:
`cortex_source(project_root, node_id)` — Returns the raw source body of a node, looked up by ID.

Uses the `level_3_location` stored in the index to locate the file and line range, then returns those source lines directly.  For function-level nodes this is a precise excerpt (`#L10-L45`); for module-level nodes the entire file is returned.

This removes the need to manually parse file paths and line offsets after a search.  The full cortex-only read flow is:

```
cortex_search(project_root, "SymbolName")  →  node_id
cortex_source(project_root, node_id)        →  source body
```

vs. the previous flow that required a `Read(file, offset, limit)` call.

**Output format:**
```
# myproject::pm.cli::run_pipeline  @ pm/cli.py#L42-L87

<source lines>
```

If the node has no recorded location an `ERROR:` string is returned, consistent with all other cortex tools.

sections.heading: Recommended Agent Patterns
sections.content:
**What cortex is good for (and what it is not)**

Cortex is a staleness and orientation tool, not a call-graph tool. The scanner emits `depends_on` edges from imports and `composes` edges from module → function definitions. It does **not** parse function call sites — so `cortex_graph(direction="in")` will not find every place a function is *called*. Use `grep_search` for call-site and usage queries.

What cortex is reliable for:
- Staleness: `cortex_check` instantly confirms which nodes are CLEAN, DESC_STALE, or STRUCTURAL_DRIFT — including flagging deprecated/archived subtrees
- Orientation: what modules exist, what is deprecated, what has drifted
- Import dependency: what imports what (`depends_on` edges)
- Documentation coverage: what code has no linked doc sections

**Recommended pattern for a cross-cutting change**

1. `cortex_check(project_root)` — summary + problems only by default; confirms nothing is already broken before you start
2. `cortex_search(project_root, "SymbolName")` — locate the node; result lines include `@ path#L10-L45` for function nodes
3. `cortex_source(project_root, node_id)` — read the source body directly; no file path parsing or offset arithmetic needed
4. `grep_search` — find call sites and construction points not captured as index edges
5. Make the change, then run `cortex build --full` from the CLI to checkpoint the new baseline (full rebuild, safe because you've already verified staleness)

For day-to-day additions of new files or functions, `cortex_build()` is sufficient — it only indexes new nodes and never touches existing baselines.

**Context cost**

`cortex_check` now defaults to `problems_only=True`, emitting a one-line summary and only non-CLEAN rows. On a clean project this is two lines total. `cortex_search` and `cortex_list` results include `@ path#L10-L45` for function nodes. `cortex_source` completes the loop: once you have a node ID, the source body is one more call — no file path parsing or offset arithmetic needed.

**Listing all functions in a file (one call)**

Instead of searching for a node ID and then calling `cortex_list(parent_id=...)`, use `cortex_list(location="file.py")` directly. The `location` parameter is a case-sensitive substring match on `level_3_location`, so partial names work. Add `node_type="function"` to restrict to functions only.

sections.heading: Staleness: Check and History
sections.content:
`cortex_check(project_root, problems_only=True)` — Reports staleness status for every node: `CLEAN`, `DESC_STALE`, `STRUCTURAL_DRIFT`, or `UNVERIFIED`.

Always emits a one-line summary first:
```
3 DESC_STALE · 1 STRUCTURAL_DRIFT · 0 UNVERIFIED · 241 CLEAN
```

With `problems_only=True` (default) only non-CLEAN rows follow — output is small enough to read directly. When all nodes are clean the output is just the summary line and `(all nodes CLEAN)`. Pass `problems_only=False` for a full inventory audit.

Also appends a DOC SECTIONS block showing stale linked documentation sections (problems_only=True shows only stale sections; False shows all).

`cortex_history(project_root, node_id, limit)` — Shows the change history for a single node newest-first. Annotates the open stale window (code changed, description not updated) with the number of days it has been open.

sections.heading: Staleness: Mark Clean
sections.content: `cortex_mark_clean(project_root, node_id, reason)` — Records an `AGENT_VERIFIED` history row on a `DESC_STALE` node. Use only after reading both the current code and documentation and confirming they are consistent. The reason string is stored and surfaced in `cortex_history` for human review before push.

sections.heading: Documentation: Write and Link
sections.content:
`cortex_write_doc(project_root, doc_json)` — Accepts a JSON string describing a documentation document, writes it to `docs/{id}.json`, and immediately indexes it. Validates that all linked `node_id` values exist; unknown IDs are returned as warnings (the write still succeeds).

`cortex_add_link(project_root, section_id, node_id)` — Appends a link from an existing doc section to a code node. Loads the JSON file, adds the link, writes it back, and re-indexes. Section IDs follow the pattern `{project_id}::docs.{doc_id}::{section_id}`.

`cortex_list_undocumented(project_root, node_type, max_results=60, offset=0)` — Lists all nodes with no inbound `documents` edge. Use this to find coverage gaps. Output starts with a `[N of M undocumented nodes]` header. Accepts `"function"` and `"module"` as `node_type` aliases.

sections.heading: Documentation: Render
sections.content: `cortex_render_doc(project_root, doc_id)` — Renders a DocJSON document as Markdown, with linked node summaries listed under each section. Each section heading is annotated with its section ID (e.g. `<!-- id: my-section-id -->`), so agents can call `cortex_update_section` immediately after reading without a separate enumeration step. Each linked function node includes a `@ path#L10-L45` suffix. Pass `doc_id="list"` to see all indexed document IDs.
